quizQuestion: Which Sith Lord, revealed to be Luke Skywalker's father in The Empire Strikes Back (1980), is one of cinema's most iconic villains?
explanation: Darth Vader is the primary antagonist of the original Star Wars trilogy. In The Empire Strikes Back (1980), he delivers the twist 'No, I am your father' — often misquoted as 'Luke, I am your father.' His body was played by David Prowse and his voice by James Earl Jones. The AFI ranked him the number three villain in cinema history. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Darth Vader's revelation 'No, I am your father' in The Empire Strikes Back is one of cinema's greatest plot twists.
wowFactor: The line is almost always misquoted — Vader never says 'Luke, I am your father.' The actual line is 'No, I am your father.'

variants.question: Darth Vader's voice in the Star Wars films was provided by which actor?
In The Empire Strikes Back, Darth Vader reveals to Luke: 'No, {___} your father.'

quizQuestion: Which fictional British spy, first portrayed by Sean Connery in Dr. No (1962), introduces himself with the iconic line 'Bond. James Bond.'?
explanation: James Bond is the fictional MI6 spy created by author Ian Fleming. The film series began with Dr. No in 1962, starring Sean Connery. The self-introduction 'Bond. James Bond.' first appeared in that film and became one of cinema's most recognisable phrases, ranked #22 on the AFI's 100 greatest movie quotes list. The character has been portrayed by seven actors in the official EON Productions series. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: James Bond first introduced himself with 'Bond. James Bond.' in Dr. No (1962), played by Sean Connery.
wowFactor: The James Bond film franchise has grossed over $7 billion worldwide, making it one of the highest-grossing media franchises in history.

variants.question: Sean Connery first played James Bond in which 1962 film?

quizQuestion: Which Kansas girl, played by Judy Garland in The Wizard of Oz (1939), clicks her ruby slippers and repeats 'There's no place like home'?
explanation: Dorothy Gale is the protagonist of The Wizard of Oz (1939), played by 16-year-old Judy Garland. She is swept to the magical land of Oz by a tornado and must find the Wizard to return home. Her ruby slippers and the phrase 'There's no place like home' are among the most recognisable symbols in cinema history. The AFI ranked 'There's no place like home' as the sixth greatest movie quote. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Dorothy Gale in The Wizard of Oz (1939) clicks ruby slippers and repeats 'There's no place like home', played by Judy Garland.
wowFactor: Judy Garland was only 16 when she played Dorothy, yet the studio bound her chest and put her in a corset to make her appear younger and more child-like.

variants.question: Dorothy Gale is the main character of The Wizard of Oz (1939). What state does she live in, and desperately wish to return to?

quizQuestion: Which Southern lawyer, played by Gregory Peck in To Kill a Mockingbird (1962), defends a Black man wrongly accused of rape and was ranked the number one hero in cinema by the AFI?
explanation: Atticus Finch is the moral centre of To Kill a Mockingbird (1962), played by Gregory Peck, who won the Best Actor Oscar for the role. The AFI named Atticus Finch the greatest hero in American film history. In the story, Atticus defends Tom Robinson, a Black man falsely accused of raping a white woman in 1930s Alabama. The character is based on novelist Harper Lee's own father, Amasa Coleman Lee. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Atticus Finch, played by Gregory Peck, was ranked the number one cinema hero by the AFI — the greatest hero in American film history.
wowFactor: The AFI ranked Atticus Finch the greatest screen hero ever — above Indiana Jones, James Bond, and Luke Skywalker.

variants.question: Gregory Peck won Best Actor for playing Atticus Finch. Which novel, written by Harper Lee, is the source material?

quizQuestion: Which cynical American expatriate, played by Humphrey Bogart in Casablanca (1942), runs a nightclub in Morocco and says 'Here's looking at you, kid'?
explanation: Rick Blaine is the protagonist of Casablanca (1942), played by Humphrey Bogart. A bitter, world-weary American who runs a café in wartime Morocco, Rick is forced to confront his past when his former lover Ilsa arrives with her husband, a Resistance leader. The phrase 'Here's looking at you, kid' was partly improvised. The AFI ranked Casablanca the second greatest American film ever made. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Rick Blaine, played by Humphrey Bogart, is the world-weary café owner at the heart of Casablanca (1942).
wowFactor: Humphrey Bogart's iconic line 'Here's looking at you, kid' was partly improvised — Bogart used it when teaching Ingrid Bergman to play poker between takes.

variants.question: Rick Blaine's café in Casablanca is named what?

quizQuestion: Which warrant officer on the spacecraft Nostromo, played by Sigourney Weaver in Alien (1979), became one of cinema's definitive female action heroes?
explanation: Ellen Ripley is the protagonist of the Alien franchise, first played by Sigourney Weaver in Alien (1979). She is the sole survivor of the Nostromo crew's encounter with the Xenomorph. Weaver's portrayal set a new standard for female protagonists in action and horror films. The AFI ranked Ripley as the eighth greatest hero in cinema history — and Weaver received an Oscar nomination for the role in the 1986 sequel, Aliens. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Ellen Ripley in Alien (1979), played by Sigourney Weaver, was ranked the eighth greatest cinema hero by the AFI.
wowFactor: Sigourney Weaver received an Oscar nomination for Aliens (1986) — a rare achievement for a performance in an action/science-fiction film.

variants.question: Ellen Ripley first appears in Alien (1979) as a crew member of which spacecraft?

quizQuestion: Which Cuban immigrant turned Miami drug lord, played by Al Pacino in Scarface (1983), delivers the line 'Say hello to my little friend!'?
explanation: Tony Montana is the protagonist of Scarface (1983), directed by Brian De Palma. Al Pacino plays the Cuban refugee who rises to become a powerful cocaine kingpin in Miami. The film was controversial on release for its graphic violence and language but became a cult classic. 'Say hello to my little friend!' is ranked by the AFI among the most memorable movie quotes. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Tony Montana in Scarface (1983), played by Al Pacino, is the Cuban immigrant who becomes a Miami cocaine kingpin.
wowFactor: Scarface (1983) used the F-word 226 times — a record at the time of release — and initially received an X rating before cuts were made to achieve an R.

quizQuestion: Which writer-turned-caretaker, played by Jack Nicholson in The Shining (1980), smashes through a door and shouts 'Here's Johnny!'?
explanation: Jack Torrance is the antagonist of The Shining (1980), Stanley Kubrick's adaptation of Stephen King's novel. Jack Nicholson plays a writer who takes his family to the remote Overlook Hotel for the winter, where supernatural forces drive him to violent madness. The line 'Here's Johnny!' was improvised by Nicholson, referencing Ed McMahon's introduction of Johnny Carson on The Tonight Show. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Jack Torrance's 'Here's Johnny!' in The Shining (1980) was improvised by Jack Nicholson, referencing The Tonight Show.
wowFactor: Jack Nicholson improvised the iconic 'Here's Johnny!' line — Kubrick had no idea what it referred to until someone explained the Tonight Show reference.

quizQuestion: Which insomniac Vietnam veteran cab driver, played by Robert De Niro in Taxi Driver (1976), stares at his reflection and asks 'You talkin' to me?'?
explanation: Travis Bickle is the protagonist of Taxi Driver (1976), directed by Martin Scorsese and written by Paul Schrader. Robert De Niro plays a disturbed, alienated Vietnam veteran who drives a taxi in New York City and becomes increasingly obsessed with cleaning up what he sees as the city's moral decay. The 'You talkin' to me?' scene was largely improvised by De Niro. The film won the Palme d'Or at Cannes. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Travis Bickle's 'You talkin' to me?' monologue in Taxi Driver (1976) was largely improvised by Robert De Niro.
wowFactor: The script gave De Niro only the stage direction 'Travis looks in the mirror' — the entire 'You talkin' to me?' speech was improvised on the spot.

quizQuestion: Which war hero who becomes a ruthless Mafia don, played by Al Pacino in The Godfather (1972), is the film's true central character despite not being named in the title?
explanation: Michael Corleone is the youngest son of patriarch Vito Corleone in The Godfather (1972), played by Al Pacino. Though initially reluctant to join the family's criminal empire, he ultimately becomes the most cold-blooded and powerful Corleone of all. Al Pacino was largely unknown when cast; studio executives initially opposed his casting. The character arc across Parts I and II is considered one of cinema's greatest. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Michael Corleone's transformation from idealistic war hero to ruthless Mafia don in The Godfather (1972), played by Al Pacino, is considered one of cinema's greatest character arcs.
wowFactor: Studio executives fought hard against casting Al Pacino as Michael Corleone, calling him too short and uncharismatic — Coppola had to battle to keep him.

quizQuestion: Which young Tatooine farm boy, played by Mark Hamill in Star Wars (1977), becomes a Jedi and destroys the Death Star?
explanation: Luke Skywalker is the hero of the original Star Wars trilogy, played by Mark Hamill. In Star Wars: Episode IV — A New Hope (1977), he goes from a frustrated moisture farmer on Tatooine to the Rebel pilot who fires the shot that destroys the Empire's Death Star. He was later revealed to be the son of Darth Vader. Mark Hamill was a relative unknown when cast; George Lucas saw over 300 actors before choosing him. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Luke Skywalker, played by Mark Hamill in Star Wars (1977), is the Rebel hero who destroys the Death Star and later learns Darth Vader is his father.
wowFactor: George Lucas screened over 300 actors before casting Mark Hamill as Luke — among those considered were Robby Benson, William Katt, and a young Kurt Russell.

quizQuestion: Which gentle alien, stranded on Earth in Steven Spielberg's 1982 film, learns to communicate by saying 'E.T. phone home'?
explanation: E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial (1982) is Steven Spielberg's story of an alien accidentally left behind on Earth who is befriended by a boy named Elliott. E.T. famously says 'E.T. phone home' as he tries to contact his home planet. The film was the highest-grossing film of all time upon release, surpassing Star Wars, and held that record for eleven years until Jurassic Park (1993). Source: Wikipedia.
statement: E.T. from E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial (1982) was the highest-grossing film of all time upon its release, surpassing Star Wars.
wowFactor: E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial (1982) was so successful that for eleven years it held the record as the highest-grossing film ever made — until Spielberg himself broke it with Jurassic Park.

quizQuestion: Which Philadelphia club boxer, played by Sylvester Stallone in Rocky (1976), trains by running up museum steps and gets a shot at the world heavyweight title?
explanation: Rocky Balboa is the title character of Rocky (1976), written by and starring Sylvester Stallone. A small-time club boxer from Philadelphia, Rocky is given a once-in-a-lifetime shot at the world heavyweight championship. The film won three Academy Awards including Best Picture. Stallone wrote the script in three and a half days after watching a Muhammad Ali fight, and refused to sell it unless he could star. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Rocky Balboa in Rocky (1976) was written by Sylvester Stallone in three and a half days — and he refused to sell the script unless he could star in it.
wowFactor: Stallone was offered $360,000 for the Rocky script but refused to sell unless he could star — at the time he had only $106 in his bank account.

quizQuestion: Which Roman general-turned-gladiator, played by Russell Crowe in Gladiator (2000), shouts 'Are you not entertained?!' to the Colosseum crowd?
explanation: Maximus Decimus Meridius is the protagonist of Gladiator (2000), directed by Ridley Scott and played by Russell Crowe. A Roman general betrayed after Emperor Marcus Aurelius's death, he is enslaved and forced to become a gladiator, seeking revenge against the corrupt Emperor Commodus. Russell Crowe won the Academy Award for Best Actor, and the film won Best Picture. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Maximus in Gladiator (2000), played by Russell Crowe, is a Roman general who becomes a slave gladiator to avenge his family's murder.
wowFactor: Russell Crowe had a genuine shoulder injury during filming Gladiator — the tense, one-armed sword fight choreography was designed around his inability to fully use his arm.

quizQuestion: Which computer hacker turned messianic hero, played by Keanu Reeves in The Matrix (1999), chooses the red pill to learn the truth about reality?
explanation: Neo is the protagonist of The Matrix (1999), directed by the Wachowskis and played by Keanu Reeves. He is a computer programmer named Thomas Anderson by day and hacker 'Neo' by night, who is contacted by Morpheus and offered a choice: take the blue pill and remain in the simulated reality of the Matrix, or take the red pill and learn the truth. The film pioneered the 'bullet time' visual effect and became a major cultural touchstone. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: Neo in The Matrix (1999), played by Keanu Reeves, chooses the red pill to escape a simulated reality, in a film that pioneered 'bullet time' visual effects.
wowFactor: Will Smith turned down the role of Neo to make Wild Wild West — and has since listed it as one of his greatest career regrets.

quizQuestion: Which famous three-word movie quote is spoken by a Terminator cyborg to a police sergeant before crashing a car through the station?
explanation: Arnold Schwarzenegger delivers 'I'll be back' as the T-800 Terminator in The Terminator (1984), directed by James Cameron. The line is spoken to a police desk sergeant before the Terminator drives a car through the station to reach Sarah Connor. Schwarzenegger initially argued for 'I will be back' but Cameron insisted on the contraction. It was ranked by the AFI as the 37th greatest movie quote. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: 'I'll be back' from The Terminator (1984) was ranked by the AFI as the 37th greatest movie quote.
wowFactor: Schwarzenegger wanted to say 'I will be back' because he felt the contraction sounded unnatural with his accent — James Cameron overruled him, and the awkwardness became iconic.

quizQuestion: Which explosive courtroom declaration, shouted by Jack Nicholson as a Marine colonel in a 1992 legal drama, became an instantly recognisable movie quote?
explanation: Jack Nicholson shouts 'You can't handle the truth!' as Colonel Nathan R. Jessup in A Few Good Men (1992), directed by Rob Reiner. The line comes during a climactic courtroom confrontation when Tom Cruise's lawyer forces him to admit to ordering a 'Code Red.' The quote is actually part of a longer speech; the preceding line is 'You want answers?' / 'I want the truth!' Rob Reiner has said the script used 'You want the truth? You can't handle the truth!' but Nicholson dropped 'You want the truth?' Source: Wikipedia.
statement: 'You can't handle the truth!' was shouted by Jack Nicholson as Colonel Jessup in A Few Good Men (1992), directed by Rob Reiner.
wowFactor: The full exchange is 'I want the truth!' / 'You can't handle the truth!' — Nicholson dropped the preceding line 'You want the truth?' making the quote even more explosive.

quizQuestion: Which gleefully terrifying line does Jack Nicholson improvise as he smashes through a bathroom door with an axe in The Shining (1980)?
explanation: 'Here's Johnny!' is spoken by Jack Nicholson as Jack Torrance in The Shining (1980), directed by Stanley Kubrick. The line was improvised by Nicholson, mimicking Ed McMahon's introduction of Johnny Carson on The Tonight Show. Kubrick initially did not understand the reference (being British) until it was explained to him. It has since become one of the most replicated lines in cinema history. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: 'Here's Johnny!' from The Shining (1980) was improvised by Jack Nicholson, referencing Ed McMahon's catchphrase from The Tonight Show.
wowFactor: Stanley Kubrick didn't understand the reference to The Tonight Show — he'd been living in England for years and thought the line was just Nicholson being manic.

quizQuestion: Which optimistic battle cry does Buzz Lightyear shout as he launches himself into the air in Toy Story (1995)?
explanation: 'To infinity and beyond!' is the catchphrase of Buzz Lightyear, voiced by Tim Allen in Toy Story (1995), Pixar's first feature film. Buzz is a deluded space ranger action figure who believes he is real. The phrase was written by the Toy Story screenwriting team. The film was the first fully computer-animated feature film and grossed $373 million worldwide. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: 'To infinity and beyond!' is Buzz Lightyear's catchphrase from Toy Story (1995), the first fully computer-animated feature film.
wowFactor: Toy Story (1995) was the first fully computer-animated feature film — before its release, conventional wisdom in Hollywood said audiences would never accept it.

quizQuestion: Which Star Wars: The Empire Strikes Back (1980) revelation — one of cinema's greatest plot twists — is commonly misquoted with 'Luke' at the start?
explanation: Darth Vader says 'No, I am your father' in The Empire Strikes Back (1980) — almost universally misquoted as 'Luke, I am your father.' The line was recorded separately by James Earl Jones (who voiced Vader) after filming; on set, David Prowse delivered a fake line so the twist would not leak. The screenwriter Lawrence Kasdan had written it as a genuine surprise even for most of the cast. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: 'No, I am your father' from The Empire Strikes Back (1980) is one of cinema's most famous misquoted lines — the word 'Luke' does not appear.
wowFactor: The 'I am your father' twist was kept so secret that even David Prowse (who physically played Vader) was given a fake line to deliver on set — only James Earl Jones knew the real one.

quizQuestion: Which defiant closing line, delivered by Scarlett O'Hara as she vows to fight on in Gone with the Wind (1939), ends one of Hollywood's most celebrated films?
explanation: 'After all, tomorrow is another day!' are the final words of Gone with the Wind (1939), spoken by Scarlett O'Hara (Vivien Leigh) as she vows to regain Tara and win back Rhett Butler. The line — also the final line of Margaret Mitchell's 1936 novel — was ranked by the AFI as the 31st greatest movie quote. The film won eight Academy Awards and remained the highest-grossing film of all time (adjusted for inflation) for decades. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: 'After all, tomorrow is another day!' ends Gone with the Wind (1939), ranked 31st on the AFI's list of greatest movie quotes.

quizQuestion: Which famous Sherlock Holmes film quote — 'Elementary, my dear Watson' — is actually NEVER spoken in Sir Arthur Conan Doyle's original stories or the earliest Holmes films, making it cinema's most famous misattribution?
explanation: 'Elementary, my dear Watson' does not appear in any of Sir Arthur Conan Doyle's original Sherlock Holmes stories. The phrase first appeared in the 1929 film The Return of Sherlock Holmes, starring Clive Brook. It was then popularised through subsequent Holmes films and parody. In the original stories, Holmes occasionally says 'Elementary' and 'My dear Watson' separately, but never combined. The phrase is considered one of cinema's greatest misattributed quotes. Source: Wikipedia.
statement: 'Elementary, my dear Watson' never appears in Arthur Conan Doyle's original Sherlock Holmes stories — it was first used in the 1929 film The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
wowFactor: One of the most famous movie quotes in history was never actually said — 'Elementary, my dear Watson' was invented for a 1929 film and falsely attributed to the original stories ever since.

explanation: Al Pacino shouts 'Say hello to my little friend!' as Tony Montana in the climactic final shoot-out of Scarface (1983), directed by Brian De Palma. He fires a grenade launcher at the attacking hitmen from the top of his staircase. The line was ranked by the AFI as one of the most memorable movie quotes. The film's climax took four weeks to shoot and was partially inspired by the 1932 original Scarface. Source: Wikipedia.

wowFactor: Scarface's climactic shoot-out — including the 'Say hello to my little friend!' moment — took four full weeks to film and required thousands of rounds of blanks.

explanation: 'E.T. phone home' is spoken by the alien E.T. in E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial (1982), directed by Steven Spielberg. E.T. uses broken English to express his longing to communicate with his home planet. The voice of E.T. was provided by Pat Welsh, a retired teacher, combined with other voice recordings. The AFI ranked 'E.T. phone home' as the 15th greatest movie quote. Source: Wikipedia.
